comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Edward Williams aka Iolo Morganwg, poet, ritualist, opium addict, and one of the brightest stars in the glittering firmament of 19th-century literary forgery, who was responsible for more of the traditions of the Druid Revival than any other person. Did he make most of it up? Of course. Does that make it somehow inadequately Celtic? Well, Iolo was a Welshman, born and raised in a Welsh-speaking village in rural Glamorganshire, and trained in the exacting disciplines of traditional Welsh poetry -- that is to say, any one of his fingernails was more Celtic than the entire American Celtic Reconstructionist scene.

But I digress.

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. 

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***
[IMAGE:1]

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I've noticed something since I took up magical practice, and I'm wondering if you've seen it too. What I'm noticing is that an awful lot of people seem to hate things which are entirely within their capacity to control, but refuse to do anything about it.

What I'm thinking in particular are the number of people I know who's homes are a completely trashed, who admit they hate it, but don't seem to notice that all it would take to fix it is some effort on their part.

What motivates this weird inability to change something which they hate?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Congratulations. You've begun to wake up out of the state of shallow sleep that most people call "being awake."

Yes, I've seen it too, over and over again. Have you tried pointing out to them, when they talk about how much they hate how trashed their home is, that they could fix it -- and if you did, did you get the blank confused look, followed by the string of excuses that never quite seem to make sense?

(It's worth noting that there are some people who live in trashed homes because they have physical or mental illnesses that get in the way of doing an adequate job of cleaning. You can tell them from the others because they offer reasons, not excuses, and the reasons do make sense.)

Most people live in a haze of half-conscious thinking, self-defeating habits, and contradictory desires, and are miserable as a result. To wake up from that is a long slow process, but infinitely worth it...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
What gets me is that so many people seem to be utterly unaware of the reality of the situation.

Yes, I have noticed that too. The one that gets me are the people who say they don't have time, but then manage to find a whole lot of time for TV watching...

I also know people can have mental/physical issues the prevent proper cleaning. It's the people who are perfectly able to do it but don't and then complain about that baffle me.

I'd also add one further category: some people live in trashed homes because there's only so much one person can do if the rest of the people living there aren't willing to change anything: such as my current circumstances, where the home I live in is a mess, but any attempt to fix it gets hit with a wide range of excuses as to why other people can't let me work on it but then never get around to it themselves.

Also, are you saying then that the people with the messy homes want it that way at some level?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In some cases, yes. People have odd comfort zones, and will maintain them by all sorts of evasive maneuvers. Have you met the kind of person who's always outraged about something, and will find an endless succession of things to be outraged about? Or the ones who are always being put upon and abused, and will actively seek out situations where they can keep being put upon and abused? it's the same principle.

replies.title: Messy homes
replies.content:
If I might add my two cent: If I look at my personal experience it might also be a mingled thing: first cleaning up and repairing things is an acquired habit - as is plopping down in front of the TV after dinner, and to change from one to the other is not always easy and sometimes even feels painful - and one likes to avoid pain. For me it´s also that empty looking rooms have the psychological effect that I think my things have been taken away from me (a residue from childhood) and I get nervous if other people willing to help clean up too much for aforesaid reason.
My (not totally conscious looked for) solution was to move to a smaller lodging and accrue everything that belonged together in one place - at one point the available space runs out and after some time I got to experience an enough feeling and now it´s much easier to let go of stuff that still could be useful. (Another thought-habit, that looses it´s usefulness over time in this as of yet affluent society.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd also add another category: some people just genuinely don't care about cleanliness.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
And another --- some people get overwhelmed by the scale of the mess that has developed, and genuinely don't know where to start and how to cut the huge mess down into bite-sized pieces that can be managed easily.

I'm like that; if I can stay on top of the cleaning I'm fine, but if I get sick or have to work a lot of hours and the mess starts to build up, I have an awful time getting up the oomph to tackle it because it's just so much when you look at it as a whole. I had to learn to break it down into steps, and do one step at a time without looking at (and being intimidated by) the whole long list of steps. If I do that, I can whittle away at it step by step and before I notice how far I've gotten, it's done, or at least much closer to done than I had expected to get.

I also keep reminding myself that any progress at getting things cleaner is meaningful progress, so I don't get stressed out by not cleaning ALL THE THINGS. That makes it easier to take a few steps when I have the time and energy, and then a few more another time, and so on until all the steps are done, without tying myself into knots in the meantime about the steps that aren't done yet.

I found the Tumblr Unf*ck Your Habitat (http://unfuckyourhabitat.tumblr.com/) incredibly valuable in learning how to break a big cleaning task into smaller pieces so I could get it done, and how to pace myself and rest at intervals so I get more done in one day.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, break it up into little tasks. Neatness in the house is one that I struggle mightily with. My parents, for many reasons, just didn't. I grew up in a house that was utter chaos in cleanliness terms, and learned zero housekeeping skills until adulthood. Been working on it ever since, and have got to where I can (mostly) maintain a house that is not perfect, but is acceptable.

The biggest thing is to start. I hate washing dishes. It's always too much. I have to tell myself: "I don't have to wash the dishes, I'll just stack them to clear the sink." And 90% of the time, once that's done, I go ahead and wash them, because now that I've started the job, it doesn't seem so overwhelming. Most other tasks are also amenable to this strategy.

To all the rest of you fighting the same fight: my sincere well-wishes! Don't give up!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: JMG, I have a quation about this: In what relationship to the development of a soul, resp. an individuality does the haze of half-conscious thinking, the self-defeating habits, and the contradictory desires exist? I assume magic is one of the tools to get out under the mentioned dysfunctions. Am I right to assume that there are other possibilities, too?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A good complex question! In occult philosophy, human beings are said to have physical, etheric, and astral bodies fully formed -- we were busy doing that in our prehuman incarnations -- but the mental body is not fully formed; some schools prefer to call it a "mental sheath" instead of a mental body, because it's still not much more than an inchoate layer of unformed or half-formed mind-stuff. In the broadest sense, the creation of a fully formed mental body is the task for which we're here as human beings. The process of gaining the ability to reflect on our thoughts and our feelings, so that we can see how we're making ourselves miserable, is a very important part of that process of creation.

Magic is one set of tools for doing that. There are many, many others.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Can you expand on what those other sets of tools might be? Some seem to say magic is the one and only way, others say alchemy, others say meditation or psychological differentiation through lived experience and reflection... the more I read about magic, the more respect and curiosity I have for it, but also the more I suspect it can't be the only way to enlightenment.

You've said before that there are many paths up the mountain, indeed, there are many different mountains to climb. But if building a mental body so as to go on to Gwynfydd is the endpoint of human existence, maybe there are better and worser ways?

- Dylan

comments.title: Prayer and Deity Names
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I have been following the guidelines from your book Paths of Wisdom for over a year now. I am taking it slow, and have had a few ‘life’ interruptions (international migration) along the way. I am slowly working my way up the tree through the meditations, with sometimes interesting insights.

More lately I have been wondering about prayer. A while ago you directed someone to pray to Hermes on a Wednesday using an orange candle. I assumed the equivalent in Hermetic Qabalah would be praying to Elohim Tzabaoth, and the orange is similarly associated with Hod etc. I guess I have many questions relating to this, but I’ll stick to three:

1. Is this choice, to focus on one name the best option? It feels like I am excluding the rest of the tree to focus only on one aspect.
2. Reading your Weird of Hali series, I am struck by the relationship the main character developed with the toad God, Tsathoggua. Is this relationship a realistic expectation, or even something you or people you know experience?
3. Are the names interchangeable in light of your recent posts on how the Weird of Hali ties up with the tree of life? Would it matter if you used Hermes instead of Elohim Tzabaoth?

Thank you for all the knowledge you share.

replies.title: Re: Prayer and Deity Names
replies.content:
Okay, we're getting into complex territory here. The version of the Tree of Life covered in Paths of Wisdom is specific to the spiritual system of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, which is what I practiced when I wrote it. Not everybody is called to work with that system, or those divine names. If you feel called to pray to Hermes, pray to Hermes, and don't worry about the Cabalistic dimension. With that in mind...

1) It's very common in modern magical spirituality for people to cultivate a relationship with one deity, who becomes their patron deity, and to work with other spiritual powers in other contexts. It's perfectly fine to focus on one divine being for your prayers, while invoking other beings in magical workings or the like.

2) Jenny's practice of reflective prayer is a real thing. How dramatic the results will be vary from person to person -- I based her experiences on the sort of thing that happens to people who have a lot of talent for that work -- but the basic sort of relationship is something that happens fairly often. (That's why I put it in the book.)

3) It's not quite accurate to say that the names are interchangeable -- Hermes and Elohim Tzabaoth are different personalities, though they express similar energies -- but you can use the same practices for each. If you feel called to pray to Hermes, and you're good with that, then by all means do so.

replies.title: Re: Prayer and Deity Names
replies.content:
I'd like to ask a related follow-up. Much like (Anonymous) above, I've been inspired by Paths of Wisdom, and particularly enjoy the last section of the book. Your points about Will, Imagination, and Memory, and their relation to Geburah, Tiphareth and Chesed have been like rocket fuel. However, in last week's Magic Monday you wrote "(I made some embarrassing howlers in books such as Paths of Wisdom -- the practical material is great, but the philosophical stuff could have used a decade or two of further study!)". I hope this wasn't the site of those howlers :D, and could you briefly sketch out where your ideas changed the most?

Secondly, I'm also enjoying the Weird of Hali. Just when I have enough hobbies, I'm interesting in getting a chafing dish !

Best,
--Michael

replies.title: Re: Prayer and Deity Names
replies.content:
The howlers in Paths of Wisdom were early on, when I was setting out the philosophical justification for the system. You remember the bit where I try to explain the gap between what we perceive and what's actually out there by pointing to photons, sense organs and the nervous system as intermediaries? Nietzsche pointed out a long time ago that that argument assumes that photons, sense organs, and the nervous system are objective realities -- when the whole point of the argument is that things we can perceive (including photons, sense organs, and the nervous system) are not objective realities!

The material in the last section of the book, by contrast, has stood up very well, and I'd still say the same things today.

Glad to hear about the chafing dish. I got that from Dion Fortune's The Sea Priestess -- the "Miss Morgan" Sylvia and Claire talk about will be familiar to fans of Fortune's fiction, and she was really good with chafing dishes. (Those books are full of Easter eggs like that, btw, mostly from the old Weird Tales authors but by no means all!)

replies.title: Re: Prayer and Deity Names
replies.content:
Aw, heck yeah, I'm glad to hear that! I remember that part only with some effort, which I hope provides a satisfied chuckle. :p

Fantastic about the Easter eggs...Of Fortune's "fiction," I've only read Dr. Taverner so far, and wish I could visit that green Isle between the wars.

One thing I'm enjoying very much right now about WoH: Kingsport, is the simple intent of the family to always provide a home for the lost grand-daughter. With regard to Kingsport, I'm glad that came across. One of the things I intended with this series is to take a sidelong look at what passes for "good" in recent fantasy fiction, and offer some alternatives...

Dear JMG!

My father died of cancer last week. His condition suddenly got worse and I felt I could not say goodbye, although I was with him at the last weeks and at his death . What should I do? replies.title: Re: mourning

Second, it's important not to interrupt him too much while he goes through the early phases of the after-death process; he has a lot of work to do. Your best bet at this stage is to pray to the deity you worship, and ask that deity to help your father. That's something deities know how to do, and they're good at it.

replies.title: Re: mourning

replies.title: Re: mourning
replies.content: You can certainly practice daily meditations. As for the early phase, the traditional length is forty days, though it really does vary from person to person.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Can one reliably work the DOGD or some other form of Golden Dawn magic while not subscribing to Neo-Platonism? Particularly, rejecting the existence of objective, independently existing universals while remaining open to the idea of emanation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Of course you can. The vast majority of people who practice Golden Dawn magic have only a minimal philosophical education if they have any at al; far from believing in the existence of objective, independently existing universals, most Golden Dawnies I know wouldn't be quite sure exactly what that means. This does not stop them from becoming capable operative mages and experiencing steady spiritual growth.

One thing, though -- how well do you know what you're disagreeing with? In classic Neoplatonism, nothing at all can be described as "independently existing"; all things other than the One exist by virtue of their direct or indirect participation in the One, while the One itself does not exist -- it is the principle through which existence is possible, but nothing, not even existence, can be predicated of it. The universals, furthermore, are intelligences as well as intelligibles; the highest, noetic aspect of each human is a being of this kind, at the bottom end of the cascade of noetic existences, and the primary universals are of course the noetic aspect of the gods and goddesses. I can point you to some Iamblichus and Proclus if you want to look further...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If you were to recommend a single book to someone with no previous familiarity, which'd it be?

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Gregory Shaw's Theurgy and the Soul. It's scholarly but readable, and provides a good basic overview.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for the response!

I guess I should read more about Neoplatonism proper rather than about the modern versions/interpretations of Platonism. That paragraph you wrote seems to indicate that Neoplatonism can't be neatly assigned to metaphysical realism as some other forms of Platonism might be. Any recommendations would be welcome!

A digression: It doesn't have much to do with Neoplatonism directly, but I am a bit wary of some of the recent "traditionalist" revivals of older philosophy, particularly Thomism. I tried to get my head around Edward Feser's presentation of Aristotelian/Thomist final causes and it just doesn't seem to make sense to me. I witnessed a very well educated online friend who, as he progressively got deeper into the traditionalist Catholic sphere, came to reject evolution because it was "metaphysically unsound". When I asked him some questions about final causes and essences (how do you distinguish between e.g. a match, the wood that forms the match, and the tree the wood came from, the seed the tree grew from, the carbon that the tree used to grow etc when assigning final causes) I was accused of arguing in bad faith when I genuinely did not understand the argument. In general I find myself inclining towards more sceptical philosophies like Indian Madhyamaka or Pyrrhonic scepticism.

On a side note, I just finished both available Weird of Hali books over two days. Can't wait for the next one!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm partial to going straight to the sources. Plotinus' The Enneads is the starting point for the entire Neoplatonist project; it's not user-friendly, and is best taken one discourse at a time with plenty of time for reflection between, but you get to see one of the great minds of classical philosophy taking on what were the major philosophical issues of his time. It's been a while since I read any of the modern works on him, but iirc John Rist's Plotinus: The Road to Reality was pretty good. For the later Neoplatonists, Gregory Shaw's Theurgy and the Soul is a fine introduction to Iamblichus, the crucial figure in the later tradition.

I've had equally unsatisfying encounters with the "traditionalist" scene, and Thomism -- well, let's just say I never did find it either convincing or appealing. As for the notion that evolution is metaphysically unsound, to my mind that kind of claim is an abuse of metaphysics. Since we as human beings have no direct access to the principles of reality, our metaphysical thought can at most be a tentative guess at the nature of things, and if a phenomenon as well documented as biological evolution conflicts with a given set of metaphysical theories, then the proper response is to discard or amend the theories, not to ignore the facts that contradict them.

I'm glad you liked the novels! I had a lot of fun writing them. I'll announce the release date of The Weird of Hali: Chorazin as soon as I hear from the publisher.

comments.title: Weird of Hali and meditation help
comments.content:
Dear John, first of all, your last post on the Eldricht Tree of Life finally made me buy Innsmouth: I’ve never been a huge Lovecraft fan, with only brief experiences in my teens of playing Call of Cthulhu, but, boy, I’m enjoying this! I’m now half-way through Kingsport and looking forward to the rest! The books so far remind me of the Charles Williams supernatural thrillers and Lewis’ That Hideous Strength (The Radiance did remind me of N.I.C.E.) and the Owen/Nyarlathotep exploits brought to mind Gaiman’s Shadow and Wednesday’s in American Gods so, in my view at least, you’re in very good company!
Now to my proper question: I’ve been doing (more or less) daily discursive meditation for about three months now and, with a few exceptions, my main issue with it seems to be physical discomfort/pain: After a few minutes of sitting still with a straight back, I feel pain at the back which makes relaxation a difficult thing to achieve. I’ve tried different changes of posture (support for feet, cushion at back, back resting against back of chair…) but struggle to settle into a relaxing position. Sometimes I can somehow will the discomfort to ‘be quiet’ and can go round the meditation theme, but find it would be far easier if I could relax better. I don’t have any serious issues with my back (apart from decades of nerdy hunching in front of a computer of course…) so I imagine I should be able to meditate by sitting still. Thinking around the theme sometimes work by distracting my attention from the discomfort, but I also feel the insights I get from this practice are not different from what I get by considering a theme while walking alone in the countryside or writing about it. So I would like to ask you and any of my fellow readers for any tips/help to achieve a more relaxed sitting meditation.
As always, my earnest gratitude for the service you provide.
Manuel

replies.title: Re: Weird of Hali and meditation help
replies.content:
Thank you! I'm delighted to hear that you're enjoying the books. The Radiance should remind you of N.I.C.E. -- the reference is deliberate. (That's why their base at Miskatonic is, ahem, Belbury Hall.)

The physical discomfort is a common experience; your body is starting to let go of some of its fixed tensions, and that's going to hurt no matter how you do it. Just keep going; the pain will increase for a while, and then it will fade away, and that part of your back will be stronger and more supple. As for the results of meditation, after three months, yeah, the insights you get should be about what you'd get while walking or writing. It takes time to go deeper.

replies.title: Re: Weird of Hali and meditation help
replies.content: Many thanks for that, I will persevere... and Belbury of course, nice one!

replies.title: Re: Weird of Hali and meditation help
replies.content:
Not JMG, but I have a suggestion that has helped me: try having a good thorough stretch before you sit down to meditate. I found that it helps to reduce the pain and tension I get during meditation because it wakes up my muscles and helps them to relax more completely in the relaxation and breathing stage of the meditation.

Hope this works for you!

comments.title: fays and stuff
comments.content:
1. I just finished reading Passport to Magonia, and I was struck by Vallee's comparison of some visual religious experiences with common fay appearances. I realize that experiences are typically interpreted as religious based on their context, but I can't help but feel that religious imagery is within the purview of fairy glamor. This is all fairly theoretical for me as I've never had such an experience that requires interpreting, but still...any thoughts on this? (For context, I am a hard polytheist most days.) Are we left with just faith and reasonable judgment concerning their actions, etc?

2. I've thought and meditated on this idea for quite awhile now but have made very little headway: is there, in occult philosophy, a hard distinction between subjects and objects? Or are there objects at all? I took your message in your Nature Spirits essays to be partly that many more things are alive and conscious than we typically think, and also that an I-Thou hermeneutic is more healthy. I keep looking for a natural endpoint to that idea. Is every little thing actually alive and possessing consciousness in a pantheistic worldview? E.g. a piece of plastic rubbish. If I speak to the rubbish, is there any reason to think I am addressing someone? My best guess is that I'd be addressing a piece of the Rubbish God whose bodies we are rapidly building.

Part of the trouble might be that I didn't quite understand your point when it came to mineral life/stones. Is consciousness present but slumbering in a stone? If it possesses a physical body does that mean it exists on all the higher planes as well?

Thanks in advance for your answers, and thank you for hosting this!

Peter Witkowski

replies.title: Re: fays and stuff
replies.content:
1) Faith, reasonable judgment...and protective magic. Methods to reveal the fay for what they are, or chase them off, are well represented in traditional folk magic. Some aspects of traditional religious practice, for that matter, are pretty clearly designed to make sure that what you're talking to in a temple is what it's supposed to be.

2) By "object" do you mean something that's only an object, and never a subject? In occult philosophy, such things don't exist. Remember that in occult philosophy, spirit is the basic "stuff" of existence, and matter is simply a portion of spirit that has become temporarily fixed in an unusually rigid form. So your stone is a portion of spirit that has condensed, for a while, into that form. Everything that exists on the material plane exists on all the other planes as well, though things can exist on the subtler planes without a material dimension -- they either haven't entered into material form yet, or have freed themselves from matter.

The way it works, according to traditional occult philosophy, is that currents of unformed, impersonal spirit flow down the planes all the way to the material plane, where they take form as "inanimate" matter. From there, the currents divide into individual monads, which become the simplest of living things and work their way up from there, gradually unfolding all the potentials of the higher planes one at a time in individual form. So your stone is a mass of unformed, impersonal spirit slowly adapting to existence in matter. As it's eroded away, portions of the spirit become beings, take on bodies, and begin the long ascent; involution gives way to evolution.

Does that clarify things a bit?

replies.title: Re: fays and stuff
replies.content:
Quite a bit, yes. On the other end of things, a corpse would be an example of something in transition in the other direction? Is there a time when there is no spirit "in the body" during this transition into soil and other animal bodies? Or is it the case that spirit constantly sustains the material, and thus when the spirit of a rabbit withdraws from her body in the days following death, some other spirit takes its place? In other words, how would you describe the spiritual "stuff" of the meat in my refrigerator?

Regarding no. 1: Of course; I should've guessed. I just never came across this before.

replies.title: Re: fays and stuff
replies.content: A corpse is going the other direction. The spirit leaves, the other bodies detach and go on to their own diverse destinies, and the matter that composes the physical body is absorbed by other living things. The meat in your refrigerator is matter in transit from the rabbit to you.  The rabbit got it from plants, after it had already been adapted to work with the energies of an incarnate being, and you're getting it after further adaptation to life energies, which is why meat is so nourishing to omnivores like you and me.

replies.title: Re: fays and stuff
replies.content:

Isn't that Gnosticism, or some of that pesky biophobia?

replies.title: Re: fays and stuff

Physical matter is a necessary part of the cosmos, and it's also necessary for us at this stage of our unfolding; eventually we'll be moving on to other forms of embodiment, not because matter is "bad" but because we'll have finished our spiritual childhood and be ready to move on to other things.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
After reading your response regarding the religious future of the right and left I did some research. Interestingly, according to Rhyd Widermuth, many people on the left actively distrust him because he's a polytheist. They conflate "polytheism = fascism" which would be more honest as "polytheism = fascism = evil," and so, more simply, "polytheism = evil". Also I read and reread your post on Là-Bas, and was struck deeply by the elegiac quality of the Yeats quote: "It was no longer the soul, self-moving and self-teaching -- the magical soul -- but Mother France and Mother Church."

Honestly, I found this movement towards turning away from alternative spiritualities disturbing, or rather, like a betrayal. Although I have ceased to associate with the political left given my class and social background I'm undoubtedly culturally liberal. I also happen to be a devotional polytheist, and spent a lot of time in leftist spaces with indoctrination because that was the only place I could find other people with some shared spirituality. To see that turn would be very emotionally jarring to say the least.

That said I actually have nothing against the Abrahamic god and, indeed, love Him. I just love other gods more! Still, something about this leaving of alternative spiritualities for mainline Christianity en masse seems to me somehow sinister, especially if homophobia becomes vogue as well. It somehow reminds me of the 1980's Reagan sell-out as done by Milllenials. Again, no offense intended towards sincere Christians -- I don't think that alternative spiritualities are inherently better than Christianity, and simply am commenting on the process, which strikes me as very deeply unbalanced.

That said, I imagine that a sincere return to the Church could indeed do a lot of people in the left good, but that said, I do not like imagining the worst aspects of the leftism combined with the worst aspects of Christianity, which would fit together so well and so dysfunctionally!

I'm very curious how you imagine the non-fluffy polytheism will work its way on to the Right en masse. My friends on the right tend to be in the exact same spiritual position as my friends on the left; they are lapsed Christians for the most part. Also what do you imagine the time frame for this to be? a decade? Less? Frankly I love the idea of lots of devotional polytheists who I could pray with, and don't care about politics in that context at all. Still the process you've sketched out utterly fascinates me and would love to read more of your thoughts on it, or books on the matter if there are ones you or other readers might suggest.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I see three things going on here.

First, distrusting anything other than hard materialisms is part of the pseudo-Marxism that has defined much of the left for most of the 20th century. Rhyd's open religiosity probably scares many of his fellow leftists in the USA. Also, they probably see it as breaking ranks.

Second, ever since the end of WW2 some influential political philosophers (such as Erich Voegelin) have been arguing that only Christian monotheism can save the world from totalitarianism. Voegelin, IIRC, thought that all sorts of occultism, esotericism and paganism were inherently anti-human, and tended toward totalitarianism (usually toward Fascism, since Communism rejected religion and occultism on principle). A revived Christian cosmopolitanism was, for Voegelin and his ilk, the only safe road forward for humanity. Much of the left also prefers cosmopolitanism, though usually some form of materialistic cosmopolitanism intead of a religious one.

And third, much recent right-wing nationalism in Europe, which expressly opposes every form of cosmopolitanism (Christian or otherwise), looks with great favor on all the recent attempts to revive the old religions of the various European nations (of the Slavs, the Balts, the Scandinavians, the Germans, the Celts, etc.) In Northern, Central and Eastern Europe, as well as many sectors of Western Europe, most of the influential revived (Neo-)Pagan religions are polytheistic, and also they are very strongly nationalistic--sometimes even racialist. This garners them much support from Right-wing Europeans -- and an equal amount of hatred and mistrust from the Left in the USA.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That all makes a lot of sense, Robert; thank you for your thoughts.  Rhyd has said as much; that he's getting in trouble for going counter to the leftist orthodoxy.  I honestly think that Christianity is a better match for the left than alternative spirituality; given the cosmopolitanism you mention as well as the Left's increasing distrust of any sort of heresy.  That said, this turn of events gives me a huge amount of emotional vertigo  given that I lived my entire young adulthood in leftist spaces that heavily emphasized alternative spirituality and occultism.  Basically I'm witnessing the total implosion of the communities where I used to orient my sense of self.  This has been happening for some time, granted, but the spiritual aspect hits me a bit harder.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I think you're right about the pseudo-Marxist (and, rarely, the genuinely Marxist) Left. There's a great deal to be said for the old thesis that Marxism is basically a secularized, materialistic development of 19th-century Christianity.

But just as there are at least two distinct, barely overlapping sorts of "Red States" in the USA [the "Old South" and the "Empty Quarter" so there are at least two distinct, barely overlapping sorts of folk in the Leftist political world.

The non-Marxist Left, the part of the Left that was drawn to the New Age and/or Neo-Paganism, has its roots not in Marxism, but in our home-grown tradition of creating alternative, women-led, magical (counter-)religions and spiritualities.

You don't find very much about older forms of this tradition in mainstream histories of religion in America, but it's an old, old current in our history. You can trace it as far back as some of the tolerant Separatists who came over on the Mayflower. (And I suspect, but cannot yet document, some of those same Separatists were influenced to some degree by the 16th-century "Family of Love" in England.)

At present the two kinds of "Leftist" seem to me to be in the early days of a suicidal fight to the death. Ugh!

replies.content:

Individual congregations (such as my ELCA Lutheran) can declare themselves as a body to be welcoming to all. "All", as in political parties, economic classes, ethnicities, genders, and sexual orientations. Don't assume that a resurgent Christianity will be homophobic.

As for proselytizing polytheism, my understanding is that sort of activity is counter to the very internal structures of classical polytheism.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't think bigotry against gay people* will become a lasting or widespread feature of the new liberal Christianity, although we're likely to see some of it, driven by current politics. Social justice activists are already starting to attack white gay men because they are after all white men -- the circular firing squad effect is well under way in social justice land at this point -- and the #walkaway movement has demonstrated that a lot of people on the populist right couldn't care less about who you sleep with so long as you're cheering for Donald Trump. That's going to drive a certain amount of backlash against gay men in particular. Over the longer term, my guess is that we'll see the large denominations embracing the "open to all" policy that, after all, a religion founded by someone who broke bread with sinners and publicans ought to follow, while smaller and more doctrinaire denominations will feed the denunciatory wallowing in self-righteousness that is one of Christianity's besetting sins.

As for the polytheism of the right, that's likely to be as much a generational shift as anything. Asatru attracts a lot of people from the same social and cultural strata as the populist Right, and some of the other polytheist faiths are beginning to catch on there as well. As Christian churches begin swinging leftward, I expect to see younger members of their congregations bailing out, and some of them will take up Asatru; as that begins to snowball, expect some of the lapsed Christians you know on the right to show up at Asatru ceremonies and decide they like the taste. It's going to take a couple of decades before the process completes, but I wouldn't be surprised to see things visibly in motion before Trump leaves office.

*I dislike the term "homophobia;" the last thing we need is another attempt to treat differences in personal beliefs as a mental illness.

In a very real sense, it's even more loaded than "racist," which is saying something! Also, I'm glad to see your thoughts on the future of Christianity, too. comments.title: Mirrors
comments.content:
Hello JMG, long time reader, have commented before on rare occasions on the main blog.

No magical experience as such (although I bought your Learning Ritual Magic book, albeit currently stowed away because it freaks out my wife).

I've been doing "normal" mindfulness meditation on and off for years. More off than on, but I have some experience with the Headspace app etc.

Recently been getting interested in discursive meditation and have started doing it after finding an old post of yours about it (only done it once or twice so far but it's fascinating just how deeply I was able to go into a concept in even ten minutes).

Anyway, my question was inspired by a comment here a week or two ago about mirrors. I think you replied to someone saying something like (I paraphrase): "Mirrors aren't magical in themselves, but some people find them useful as a tool to visualize and enter into a light trance state".

Ever since I was a kid, long before I knew anything about magic etc, I occasionally got the strangest feeling when I look into a mirror when I'm alone, within a few seconds. Not every time or even close to it, but when it's quiet and I'm alone etc, has happened moderately often, reliably for decades. It's hard to describe , but kind of like my consciousness is detached from myself, from my identity as "me". Not physically detached , it's all within my body, but I get the strangest sensation of my "self" or consciousness observing "me" - the person/identity who bears my name and bears all the desires/worries/concerns of my life as a separate entity - like my consciousness is distinct from my identity and cares and worries if that makes sense. It lasts 10-30 seconds usually and then kind of "reintegrates" till I'm fully me again and I'm feeling all the usual cares of life, my mortgage and kids and what have you..(I never felt anything like this while meditating - only when looking into mirrors).

I guess my question(s) are:

1. Am I imagining this?
2. Is this the kind of trance state you were talking about with mirrors, and if not, what could it be?
3. Is this (mental state) useful for meditation or any other purpose? What can I do "there" if that makes sense?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Mirrors
replies.content:
1) No. Some people have to put years of hard work into learning to achieve that state.

2) It's one of several kinds of trance you can get from mirrors.

3) It's very useful. The "you" you experience in that state is who you really are; the identity and cares and worries are your lower self, the self you've put on to enter into incarnation. Practice it regularly and a range of benefits emerge. You're likely to find your intuition improving steadily; you may, after a time, begin to get glimpses of previous lives; when your physical body dies, you'll have a much easier time navigating the after-death state; and you may find the state opening up into a range of other magical or mystical states. I'd encourage you to consider doing it once a week, and see where it takes you.

replies.title: Re: Excess of element
replies.content:
Your natal horoscope is what it is to balance what you've been and done in previous lives. If you've got most of the planets in signs of one element, that says you need to work on that element a lot. Of course other factors can help balance it out -- but what looks unbalanced in the context of one life may well be a balancing move seen in the context of many lives.

The elements are in some sense more basic than the planets. You're always going to have all the planets present in your chart, but your elemental balance can vary all over the map, and it usually is what it is and won't change over the course of a life.

replies.title: Re: Excess of element
replies.content: If you've resisted fire the most, yeah, you need to come to terms with its positive aspects and learn to express it honestly and clearly in your life and actions. Exactly what that involves is something nobody else can tell you; I'd suggest meditating and journaling on it.

comments.title: Several  questions
comments.content:
from Goats and Roses

You have advised people many times not to combine or mix and match elements of different magical systems. You have also said that you designed CGD and tried it out to make sure that it was a "workable system." What makes a system of magic "workable?" What outcomes would define that? Do all workable systems have the same outcomes as an end goal?

I am doing my best to try to vibrate words as directed. However I have no idea really if I am doing this effectively. How will I know? What part of the body does the vibration occur in? Obviously I can feel it in my mouth and chest because that's where the air is. Is there anywhere I can see this demonstrated?

Not a question but an anecdote. I am working with geomancy. I was puzzled by readings and realized I really didn't have a feel for the astrological houses and what areas of life were relevant to each one. So I started doing a discursive meditation every day, one house at a time. Each one seemed to yield insights, but when I had gotten halfway around the chart, in that meditation I got a whiff of the "flavor" of that house, and all the ones previous. It was like a shift in the type of information. Much richer, multidimensional and quite unmistakeable. It was like smellling garlic and then roses, unmistakeable. I am now in love with discursive meditation. TSW!

Thank you for MM and everything else that you do.

replies.title: Re: Several  questions
replies.content:
1) Different systems have different goals and purposes. What I mean by "workable" is that (a) a student should be able to proceed successfully through the entire system by steady hard work, without getting tripped up by internal barriers; (b) the results of practicing the system should include a gain in physical, emotional, mental, and spiritual health; and (c) the system should do what it claims to be able to do -- if the book says you'll be able to work certain kinds of magic effectively, a student should reliably be able to do just that after working with it.

2) No two people I know sound quite the same while vibrating. Don't worry about it; if you're getting a vibrating feeling in your throat and chest, that's great; keep practicing and you'll get the hang of directing the vibration to other parts of your body, and then to points outside your body.

3) TSW indeed! Delighted to hear it.

Oog. Yes, every spiritual movement has its vulgarizers, those who take the ideas and techniques and debase them into cheap manipulative gimmicks that feed the bad habits of the lower self. Napoleon Hill and Dale Carnegie are examples of that sort of thing in the trad New Thought movement, Tony Robbins is a great example of the recent equivalent.

The Order of Essenes teachings I've made available online differ from that in that sort of debased teaching in the way that actual spiritual teachings always differ from their vulgarizations -- by retaining the awareness that the point of the work is entering into a healthy and balanced relationship with the cosmos, not engaging in cheap manipulations to get the ego what it thinks it wants. Meditation, discursive as well as the distinctive New Thought practice of "entering the silence" (basically Zen shikan-taza in Western drag), are essential to the OE teachings, and so is the practice of generosity, focusing on what you can give rather than what you can get.

As for the habit of recruitment -- agreed. Any time an organization focuses on recruitment, you know the organization is more interested in what it can do for itself than in what it can do for you. Not incidentally, that was why the OE never advertised, relying on word of mouth to attract students, and never charged for its lessons. (I've kept both these habits in place in the online relaunch of the course.)

In a previous Magic Monday, you responded to the suggestion of an Elder Gods Golden Dawn with the idea that Renaissance magic would be more appropriate. (Assuming I understood the answer.) Could you expand a bit on what that might entail?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Delighted to hear it. I got really tired a long time ago of those characters so heavily overrepresented in fantasy, the oversensitive angst-ridden adolescents who happen to be the Ultimate Special Snowflake who alone can wield the Ultimate Special Power blah blah blah, so I put a relatively ordinary guy with relatively ordinary concerns and capacities at the center of this narrative. When we do get someone who really does have some remarkable gifts, she's a bookish, somewhat shy, not particularly overemotional young woman whose sorcerous gifts are really the only remarkable thing about her. (Well, that and she's got a very quick mind. She's also asexual, but that's not all that unusual.) The other characters in the series, whether I invented them (Miriam Akeley, for example, or the ones who haven't shown up yet) or the ones Lovecraft invented (Charles Dexter Ward and Randolph Carter play significant roles in later books), are similar: relatively ordinary people put into extraordinary situations and trying to cope. That's the kind of story I like, so my readers will have to put up with it. ;-)

2) You probably won't be able to get them on the shelves; WoH is published by a small print-on-demand firm that isn't really set up to sell wholesale, and most bookstores won't touch those. The only novel of mine that was published by a larger publisher is Twilight's Last Gleaming, and that's being re-released this year in a slightly revised edition.

3) The sorcery in WoH is a free mix of actual occultism and stuff from Lovecraft, Clark Ashton Smith, and other classic authors of the weird tale. The Mao games, the games Jenny learns how to play in Kingsport, come from Arthur Machen's "The White People" (as do the White, Green, and Red Ceremonies); the practice of reflective prayer she learns from Sylvia is quite real, while the Sigil of Cykranosh and the Lesser Warding of Haon-Dor are invented out of whole cloth. Don't worry about the magic; it's there, as in fantasy generally, for literary effect rather than for instructional purposes. If you want to learn actual magic, I'm sure I can recommend a book or two! ;-)

comments.title: engaging with scripture--thank you

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I used to attend a lot of public Pagan rituals at festivals and similar gatherings, but I haven't made it to many in the last couple of years for work-related reasons. I'd like to get involved with public Pagan activities again, but now I have a new concern relating to the political magic you've discussed previously.

I've heard reports that it's become somewhat common for people to interrupt non-political public rituals with impromptu political speeches or even attempt to direct the ritual toward leftward political goals.

I would like to avoid involvement with such things and, of course, keep out of any blowback that may be accrued.

How far do I need to go to avoid getting sticky when someone else starts spreading raspberry jam? Is quietly not assenting enough to stay out of it? Would something like the Druid's Egg from the DH be necessary? Or should I just avoid public rituals altogether until things cool down?

To be clear, I'm concerned about both cases where random attendees at things like sumbles use their time with the horn to preen their virtue and cases where ritual facilitators themselves neglect to mention their political intentions until after everyone's been instructed not to step outside of the circle.

My magical background: DA student in an early grade

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a tough one. In your place I'd simply avoid the whole scene, but that may or may not be suitable for your needs. The Druid's Egg would be helpful, and you might also address the gods and goddesses being invoked, and apologize to them for the bad manners of the people who are trying to drag them into petty human politics. I don't recommend doing a silent working to disable the magic from within until you have a little more knowledge -- though once you've got that, it's a very effective response.

And ritual facilitators who don't let people know that a ritual's going to be about politics until the circle's been closed should be flogged. I mean that quite literally.

replies.title: avoiding politics during rituals
replies.content:
The scene is changing and differentiating rapidly; any general statement I make about mores will have exceptions. That said, people and groups that put on rituals on a regular basis often have a website that contains statements about the group's focus and goals, and/or a page of guidelines on etiquette or expected behavior for people attending their rituals. This information can give you some clues about what to expect.

It also used to be the case, and perhaps still is, that many newbies would have all sorts of concerns and reservations about attending a public Pagan ritual, and experienced leaders would know that part of their job is to answer questions in advance, even if they privately think the question is off the wall.

Some groups don't mind spectators among their guests.

If you are attending a large festival or conference, there is no telling what you might encounter during a group ritual. If an established group or consortium of like-minded groups is putting on rituals in town on a scheduled basis, it is usually possible to find out something about what they allow or encourage before you show up. This isn't foolproof, but research can at least weed out some that are unsuitable for you. If the group are relatively inexperienced, you take your chances.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"...cases where ritual facilitators themselves neglect to mention their political intentions until after everyone's been instructed not to step outside of the circle. "

I would think that if this were the situation, any attendee would be perfectly justified in breaking that circle and leaving. At the very least.

comments.title: memory art
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
To your knowledge, has anyone created a Lullian memory system using Druid symbolism?

The thought struck me today that a person might be able to do so with the eight stations of the Wheel of the Year, and the 25 Ogham fews (or the Coelbren runes, but I'm unfamiliar with those).
Myself, I would want to place Koad at the center, as linchpin to the whole affair and anchor to the Three Currents of spirit.
-Cliff

replies.title: Re: memory art
replies.content:
The Ogham was almost certainly a mnemonic system originally. The reason the classic Ogham texts in the Auraicept na n-Eces tell you to invent new ogham sequences, using the same alphabetical sequence as the fews, is that each of these becomes a set of anchor-images for mnemonic use. (Let's say you've invented an English language animal Ogham: bear, lion, newt, fox, snake, and so on; you memorize that, and then handle your grocery list by having a bear holding a carton of milk, a lion pawing at a bag of flour, and so on.) Mary Carruthers' The Book of Memory discusses this form of mnemonic system in some detail.

As for a Lullian system, it could certainly be done, but the only thing like it I know of is in the Dolmen Arch material, where the seven cantrefs (the basic set of symbols in the system) are assigned to the concepts being, becoming, source, substance, power, purpose, and value, and the questions whether, what, how, who, where, when, and why. It makes a nice crisp combinatorial system with some interesting applications -- including a magical game played with dominoes -- but it's rather simpler than what you have in mind. I'd encourage you to go ahead with the project you've envisioned and make it happen.
